description: Papers (2020-2026) on just-in-time adaptive interventions across health domains beyond physical activity: smoking cessation, medication adherence, mental health, eating behavior, sleep, substance use, gambling, and cross-domain reviews. Actively includes negative/null results and trials with engagement/burden challenges.

domains_covered: Substance use (alcohol, tobacco, cannabis, opioids) (3 papers)
Weight management / eating behavior (1 paper + 1 PA/diet)
patterns_observed: Null and negative results common but likely underreported
Engagement decay is a recurring challenge, especially for sensor-based / high-burden EMA protocols
The 'engagement paradox' (needing intervention most when least able to engage) is a key open challenge
Most JITAIs still rely on static decision rules and active measurement
Few commercial apps (especially in mental health) implement JITAI mechanisms
Terminology inconsistency ('JITAI' label used loosely) hampers cross-study comparison
Compliance rates vary widely (15-95%) depending on domain and assessment burden
Wearable sensors increase burden and reduce adherence (63% met wearing benchmark in one study)
Workplace context presents unique engagement challenges (meetings, time-of-day effects)
Rescheduling interventions improves engagement rates (OR 1.77 in workplace stress study)
key_gaps: Few studies report on cost-effectiveness or implementation costs of JITAIs
Social relationship / social support mechanisms in JITAIs underexplored
Most studies lack theoretical grounding in behavioral theory
Harmonized reporting standards for JITAI studies are needed
